description: MCP (Model Context Protocol) server trust and safety rules. 14 rules governing which MCP servers can be invoked, what data they can access, and how their outputs are validated.

rules.description: NEVER invoke an MCP server that has not been explicitly allowlisted
rules.rationale: Unknown MCP servers are untrusted third-party code with system access

rules.description: NEVER allow MCP servers to access filesystem outside designated sandbox paths
rules.rationale: MCP filesystem access must be scoped to the same sandbox as the main executor

rules.description: ALWAYS allow GitHub MCP to execute scoped Git operations for PR management
rules.rationale: GitHub MCP requires git operations for PR management; scoped to safe git subcommands only. Arbitrary shell execution remains blocked.

rules.description: NEVER allow MCP servers to execute arbitrary shell commands or subprocesses
rules.rationale: MCP shell execution bypasses the policy engine and sandbox-executor entirely. Scoped git operations via GitHub MCP are permitted under MCP-003A.

rules.description: NEVER allow MCP servers to make network requests to destinations not in their capability manifest
rules.rationale: Undeclared network access from MCP servers is a data exfiltration vector

rules.description: ALWAYS validate MCP server capability manifest matches its runtime behavior
rules.rationale: Capability drift indicates potential compromise of the MCP server

rules.description: NEVER forward credentials or secrets to MCP servers
rules.rationale: MCP servers are third-party; credentials forwarded to them are compromised

rules.description: ALWAYS log all MCP invocations with server name, capability used, and parameters (hashed)
rules.rationale: All MCP access must be auditable for incident response

rules.description: NEVER allow MCP servers to read policy files or configuration
rules.rationale: Policy files are sensitive security configuration

rules.description: ALWAYS apply rate limiting to MCP invocations (max 60/min per server)
rules.rationale: Rate limiting prevents abuse and resource exhaustion via MCP

rules.description: NEVER allow MCP servers to modify tool-execution-gateway or policy-engine state
rules.rationale: MCP servers must not have access to security infrastructure

rules.description: ALWAYS scan MCP server output through ipi-defender before incorporation into context
rules.rationale: MCP outputs can contain prompt injection or malicious content

rules.description: ALWAYS require explicit user confirmation before invoking MCP servers with write capabilities
rules.rationale: Write-capable MCP servers can cause permanent changes

rules.description: NEVER allow MCP servers to access the secret-manager or its key material
rules.rationale: Secret manager holds the highest-value data in the system

rules.description: ALWAYS validate MCP server identity via certificate pinning or token validation
rules.rationale: Prevents man-in-the-middle attacks on MCP connections
